region_of_origin: Central Asia (widely cultivated in Europe and North America)
food_subgroup: Fresh fruit (botanically a vegetable; culinarily classified as fruit)

serving.natural_language_portion: One medium raw rhubarb stalk weighs approximately 51 g and provides around 11 kcal, making it a very low-calorie addition to recipes or snacks.

nutrient_source.title: Rhubarb, raw — USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (FDC ID 167758)
nutrient_source.organization: United States Department of Agriculture (USDA) Agricultural Research Service
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: USDA SR Legacy entry for raw rhubarb used as primary nutrient reference. Singapore HPB values for energy, protein, fat, carbohydrate, sugar, sodium, dietary fibre, potassium, calcium, and iron are consistent with USDA data and have been cross-validated. USDA values used for saturated fat, monounsaturated fat, polyunsaturated fat, trans fat, cholesterol, vitamin C, and other micronutrients not reported by HPB.

gi_source.title: Rhubarb GI estimated from analogous low-carbohydrate vegetable/fruit category — University of Sydney GI Database reference for low-GI non-starchy plant foods
gi_source.source_match_notes: Rhubarb does not appear as a directly tested food in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI is estimated at approximately 15 (low) based on its very low carbohydrate content (4.54 g/100g), very low sugar (1.1 g/100g), and high water content (93.6 g/100g), consistent with the GI range of analogous non-starchy, high-fibre, low-sugar plant foods (e.g., celery, cucumber) documented in the University of Sydney GI Database. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: GL = (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (15 × 2.31) / 100 ≈ 0.35, rounded to 0.3 for a 51 g stalk.

hypertension.key_factor: Very low sodium (4 mg/100g) and high potassium (288 mg/100g) with calcium (86 mg/100g) supporting DASH-aligned blood pressure management
hypertension.guidance: Raw rhubarb is an excellent choice for hypertensive patients due to its very low sodium content and high potassium-to-sodium ratio; however, patients should avoid sweetened rhubarb preparations (jams, syrups, pies) which may contain added sodium and high sugar loads that undermine blood pressure control.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Very low carbohydrate (4.54 g/100g), low sugar (1.1 g/100g), low estimated GI (~15), and 1.8 g dietary fibre per 100g providing modest glycaemic buffering
type2_diabetes.guidance: Raw rhubarb is suitable for people with Type 2 Diabetes given its very low glycaemic load; patients should be counselled that rhubarb is almost always consumed with added sugar in culinary preparations — use sugar-free sweeteners or limit added sugar to preserve its low-GI benefit, and avoid rhubarb crumbles, pies, and jams.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Very low saturated fat (0.06 g/100g), zero trans fat, zero cholesterol, high potassium (288 mg/100g), and polyphenols including anthocyanins and stilbenoids with documented cardioprotective properties
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Raw rhubarb is cardioprotective in its natural form — its negligible saturated fat, zero cholesterol, high potassium, and polyphenol content support cardiovascular health; patients should avoid high-sugar rhubarb preparations which can raise triglycerides and undermine cardiovascular benefit.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: High oxalate content in stalks; rhubarb leaves are toxic (oxalic acid); polyphenols and vitamin C provide anti-inflammatory antioxidant support
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Rhubarb stalks may be consumed in moderation by RA patients for their vitamin C and polyphenol content, but high oxalate levels warrant caution in patients with kidney stones or those on methotrexate — consult prescriber before regular consumption; rhubarb leaves must never be consumed as they contain toxic levels of oxalic acid.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Rhubarb (Rheum rhabarbarum / Rheum x hybridum) is a perennial plant whose long, celery-like stalks are the only edible part — the leaves contain toxic levels of oxalic acid and must never be consumed. The stalks range from deep crimson to pale green, with a crisp, fibrous texture and an intensely tart, sour flavour that makes raw rhubarb almost unpalatable without sweetening. Culinarily classified as a fruit despite being botanically a vegetable, rhubarb is most commonly stewed, baked into pies and crumbles, or made into jams and compotes, almost always with added sugar. Nutritionally, raw rhubarb is exceptionally low in calories (21 kcal/100g), very low in carbohydrates (4.54 g/100g) and sugar (1.1 g/100g), and provides a useful amount of potassium (288 mg/100g), calcium (86 mg/100g), vitamin C (8 mg/100g), and dietary fibre (1.8 g/100g). Its sodium content is negligible (4 mg/100g), making it naturally compatible with DASH and low-sodium dietary patterns. The estimated glycaemic index is very low (~15), and glycaemic load per standard serving is minimal. Rhubarb contains polyphenols including anthocyanins (responsible for its red pigmentation) and stilbenoids with documented anti-inflammatory and antioxidant properties. It is suitable for people managing Type 2 Diabetes, hypertension, and cardiovascular disease in its unsweetened form, but the near-universal culinary practice of adding large amounts of sugar significantly alters its nutritional profile. Rhubarb is plant-based, vegan, gluten-free, dairy-free, halal, and kosher. It is widely cultivated in temperate regions of Europe, North America, and Central Asia, and is commonly used in Western dessert traditions.
common_user_queries: Can I eat rhubarb if I have high blood pressure?
Is rhubarb a fruit or vegetable?
What is the glycaemic index of rhubarb?
Is rhubarb good for heart health?

confidence_reason: Core macronutrients sourced from Singapore HPB database (confidence 0.95) and cross-validated against USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (FDC ID 167758) for raw rhubarb. Micronutrient values sourced directly from USDA SR Legacy. GI is an analogous estimate from University of Sydney GI Database category for low-carbohydrate non-starchy plant foods, clearly documented. All values are internally consistent and plausible for raw rhubarb.
notes: 1. Cholesterol_mg is 0.0 — confirmed zero per USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy FDC ID 167758; rhubarb is a plant food and naturally contains no cholesterol. Vitamin_b12_mcg is 0.0 — naturally zero as rhubarb is a plant food with no B12. Vitamin_d_mcg is 0.0 — confirmed negligible/zero per USDA SR Legacy. Trans_fat_g is 0.0 — naturally zero in raw plant foods. 2. Monounsaturated_fat_g (0.03), polyunsaturated_fat_g (0.09), saturated_fat_g (0.06) sourced from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy FDC ID 167758 for raw rhubarb; not reported in HPB source. 3. GI is an analogous estimate (~15, low) based on University of Sydney GI Database category for low-carbohydrate, high-water, non-starchy plant foods; rhubarb has not been directly tested in the Sydney GI Database as of 2026. 4. Rhubarb leaves are toxic due to high oxalic acid content — only stalks are edible. 5. The keto dietary tag from the input record is retained as rhubarb's net carbohydrate content (~2.7 g/100g after fibre) is compatible with ketogenic dietary thresholds. 6. FoodOn URI, FoodEx2 code, and SNOMED CT code are defensible standard ontology anchors for rhubarb; reviewers should verify FoodEx2 code A00LC against the current FoodEx2 browser if updating. 7. No -1 sentinel values remain in per_100g; all fields have been populated from USDA SR Legacy or set to confirmed 0.0 where appropriate.
